title: Deterministic vs. Stochastic Sampling
concept: DDIM sampling is deterministic — the same starting noise always produces the same output, unlike DDPM's inherently random step-by-step process.

exercises.prompt: If you flip a coin, you can't predict the exact result every time. But if a robot always follows the exact same recipe with the exact same starting ingredients, what happens?

options.text: It always makes the exact same dish
It makes something random every time

exercises.explain: A fixed recipe with fixed starting ingredients always gives the same result — that's the idea behind 'deterministic'!

cards.title: Same Start, Same Picture?
cards.body: Imagine typing the exact same prompt into an AI art generator, with the exact same random noise to start... would you always get the exact same image? With some AI samplers, YES! With others, NO. Let's find out why.

cards.title: Deterministic vs. Stochastic
cards.body: DDIM sampling is deterministic: the same starting noise ALWAYS produces the same final image, step after step, with no randomness added. DDPM sampling is stochastic: it adds a little bit of random noise at EVERY step, so even the same starting noise can lead to slightly different results each time.

cards.title: Following Footprints vs. Wandering
cards.body: DDIM is like walking along a single, exact path drawn on a map — you always end up at the same destination. DDPM is like walking toward that destination but taking a slightly different wobbly path each time because a friend keeps nudging you randomly. You might still arrive near the same place, but the journey (and sometimes the exact ending spot) changes each time.

cards.title: Same Noise, Twice
cards.body: Say you start an image generator with 'noise pattern #42' both times. Using DDIM, you get the exact same cat picture both times. Using DDPM, you might get two different-looking cats, because DDPM rolls little random 'dice' at every denoising step along the way.

cards.title: Myth: All AI Image Generators Are Random
cards.body: Not true! While many diffusion models CAN be random (stochastic, like DDPM), samplers like DDIM remove that randomness on purpose. This makes DDIM predictable, faster, and great for reproducing the exact same image again if needed.

cards.title: Predictable Path vs. Random Walk
cards.body: Compare how each sampling method behaves when you repeat it with the same starting noise.

left.text: Same noise in → exact same image out, every single time. No randomness during steps.

right.text: Same noise in → slightly different image out each time, because random noise is added at every step.

options.text: It uses more random noise than other methods
The same starting noise always leads to the same final image
It skips the denoising steps entirely

exercises.explain: Deterministic means predictable and repeatable — same input, same output, every time, which is exactly how DDIM works.

exercises.prompt: DDPM sampling adds random noise at every denoising step, which can make results slightly different each time.
exercises.explain: True! DDPM's step-by-step process is stochastic, meaning it involves randomness at each step, unlike DDIM.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: DDIM sampling is called ___ because the same starting noise always produces the same output.
exercises.explain: Deterministic means the process is fixed and repeatable — no randomness is added during DDIM's steps.

exercises.prompt: You run the same starting noise through an image generator three times and get three different-looking outputs each time. Which sampling method was most likely used?

options.text: DDPM, because it adds randomness at every step
DDIM, because it never changes anything
Neither, because all samplers always give identical results

exercises.explain: Getting different results from the same starting noise is a sign of a stochastic process like DDPM, which injects randomness at each denoising step.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Since DDIM removes randomness from the sampling steps, it means DDIM images always look boring and identical to each other, no matter what prompt you use.' What is wrong with this statement?

options.text: Nothing is wrong — DDIM always produces the exact same image no matter what noise or prompt you give it
DDIM removes randomness WITHIN the same fixed starting noise, but different starting noise or different prompts still produce different images
DDIM actually adds MORE randomness than DDPM, so results are never repeatable
DDIM doesn't generate images at all, it only classifies them

exercises.explain: Deterministic sampling only means that for a GIVEN fixed starting noise, the output is always the same. Change the starting noise or prompt, and you'll still get varied, creative images — DDIM just removes the extra randomness added during the denoising steps themselves.
